title: Cell structure
summary: Cell structure is where the whole of biology gets its foundation: the idea that every living thing is built from cells, and that a cell's job is written into its parts. This lesson builds the complete A2.2 toolkit — cell theory and its evidence, the sharp divide between prokaryotic and eukaryotic organisation, the main organelles and what each one does, the specific differences between plant and animal cells, the microscopy skills of calculating magnification and actual size from a scale bar, and the surface-area-to-volume ratio that sets a hard ceiling on how large a cell can grow. Every worked example is laid out exactly the way our marking engine awards Paper 2 marks — point by point, with error-carried-forward — so you can see how showing your working protects your score.
learningObjectives: State the tenets of cell theory and describe the evidence and exceptions that shaped it.
Distinguish prokaryotic from eukaryotic cell structure, including the presence or absence of membrane-bound organelles.

Identify the main organelles from a micrograph and state the function of each (nucleus, ribosomes, rough and smooth ER, Golgi apparatus, mitochondrion, chloroplast, vacuole, cell wall, plasma membrane).
Compare the structures of plant and animal cells.
Calculate magnification and actual size from a scale bar, keeping units consistent.
Explain, using the surface-area-to-volume ratio, why cell size is limited, and present the reasoning the way marks are awarded.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL at this level.

simpleExplanation.title: The Cell: A City Within
simpleExplanation.summary: A cell is not a simple blob — it is an organised space where different jobs happen in different places. A cell's structure is a direct clue to its function, and its size is limited by a simple geometric fact: as things get bigger, their outside grows more slowly than their inside.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Picture a small village with a single shared fire pit and everyone working in one open space — that is a prokaryotic cell, simple and fast. Now picture a modern city with dedicated districts: a power station (mitochondrion), workshops that build proteins (ribosomes and rough ER), a packaging-and-postal depot (Golgi apparatus), and a walled city hall holding all the master plans (nucleus). Dividing the work into separate districts lets the city run many jobs at once without them interfering — that is compartmentalisation in a eukaryotic cell. And just as a city can only supply as many people as its roads can feed, a cell can only survive if its surface can supply the volume inside it.
steps: First, fix the ground rules — cell theory — and see why every organism is built from cells.
Next, separate the two great classes of cell: simple prokaryotes with no membrane-bound organelles, and compartmentalised eukaryotes.
Then learn the organelles by their jobs, and note which ones distinguish a plant cell from an animal cell.

Finally, master the two quantitative skills examiners test: calculating magnification and actual size from a scale bar, and using the surface-area-to-volume ratio to explain why cells cannot grow indefinitely large.

flashcards.back: 1) All living organisms are composed of one or more cells. 2) The cell is the smallest unit of life. 3) All cells arise from pre-existing cells (by division). These are the founding generalisations of cell biology.

flashcards.front: Prokaryotic vs eukaryotic — the key difference
flashcards.back: Eukaryotic cells have a membrane-bound nucleus and other membrane-bound organelles (compartmentalisation); prokaryotic cells have NO membrane-bound organelles and their DNA lies free in the cytoplasm in a region called the nucleoid.
A single circular chromosome of 'naked' DNA (not wound around histone proteins) in the nucleoid — not enclosed by a membrane. Small extra loops called plasmids may also be present.

Stores the cell's DNA (linear chromosomes) and controls cell activities by regulating which genes are transcribed. Bounded by a double membrane (nuclear envelope) with pores; contains the nucleolus, which makes ribosomes.

Site of protein synthesis (translation). Found in ALL cells — free in the cytoplasm or attached to rough ER. Eukaryotes have 80S ribosomes in the cytoplasm; prokaryotes (and mitochondria and chloroplasts) have smaller 70S ribosomes.
Rough ER is studded with ribosomes and synthesises and modifies proteins for secretion or membranes. Smooth ER has no ribosomes and synthesises lipids and steroids, and detoxifies substances.
Modifies, sorts and packages proteins and lipids received from the ER, then releases them in vesicles for secretion or delivery elsewhere in the cell.

Site of aerobic respiration, producing most of the cell's ATP. Has a double membrane; the inner membrane is folded into cristae to increase surface area. Contains its own 70S ribosomes and circular DNA.

Site of photosynthesis. Double membrane; contains stacked thylakoid membranes (holding chlorophyll) and a fluid stroma. Has its own 70S ribosomes and circular DNA. Found in plants and algae, NOT in animal cells.

A membrane-bound sac. Plant cells usually have one large permanent central vacuole (bounded by the tonoplast) that stores cell sap and maintains turgor pressure. Animal cells may have small, temporary vacuoles.

flashcards.front: Cell wall — where and made of what?
flashcards.back: A rigid layer OUTSIDE the plasma membrane that gives shape and support. Plants: cellulose. Fungi: chitin. Bacteria: peptidoglycan. Animal cells have NO cell wall.
Partially permeable boundary of every cell that controls what enters and leaves. A phospholipid bilayer with embedded proteins. Present in all cells (prokaryotic and eukaryotic, plant and animal).

flashcards.front: Plant vs animal cell — the differences
flashcards.back: PLANT cells have a cellulose cell wall, chloroplasts and a large central vacuole. ANIMAL cells have none of these but may contain centrioles. Both have a nucleus, mitochondria, ribosomes, ER, Golgi and a plasma membrane.

$\text{magnification} = \dfrac{\text{image size}}{\text{actual size}}$. Rearranged: actual size = image size ÷ magnification. Magnification has NO units. Always convert image and actual size to the SAME unit first.
1 mm = 1000 μm, 1 μm = 1000 nm, so 1 mm = 1 000 000 nm. Convert everything to one unit (μm is usually easiest) before calculating. A missed conversion is the most common calculation error.

As a cell grows, its volume rises faster than its surface area, so SA:V DECREASES. Because exchange with the environment happens across the surface but demand depends on the volume, a large cell cannot exchange materials fast enough — this limits cell size.
Never divide image size by actual size while they are in different units. Convert both to the same unit first (e.g. mm → μm) or your magnification will be wrong by a factor of 1000 or more.
SA:V gets SMALLER as a cell gets bigger, not larger. Saying the ratio increases with size reverses the whole argument and loses the mark.

quickCheck.prompt: An image of a cell is 40 mm across; the cell's actual width is 20 μm. The magnification is:
options: 800×, because you must not convert units
2000×, converting 40 mm to 40 000 μm then dividing by 20 μm
quickCheck.answer: 2000×, converting 40 mm to 40 000 μm then dividing by 20 μm

quickCheck.prompt: As a cell increases in size, its surface-area-to-volume ratio:
options: Increases, because bigger cells have more surface
Decreases, because volume grows faster than surface area
Stays the same, because both grow together
quickCheck.answer: Decreases, because volume grows faster than surface area

quickCheck.prompt: Which feature is present in a eukaryotic cell but NEVER in a prokaryotic cell?
Which structure is found in BOTH prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells?
A student writes 'plant cells have a cell wall, so animal cells have no membrane'. The error is that:
options: Animal cells do have a plasma membrane; it is the cell WALL they lack
Plant cells have no plasma membrane
Animal cells have a cellulose wall
quickCheck.answer: Animal cells do have a plasma membrane; it is the cell WALL they lack

faq.q: What is the difference between a cell wall and a plasma membrane?
faq.a: They are two separate structures with different jobs. The plasma membrane is a thin, partially permeable phospholipid bilayer found in EVERY cell; it controls what enters and leaves. The cell wall is a thicker, rigid layer OUTSIDE the membrane that provides shape and support and is only found in plants (cellulose), fungi (chitin) and bacteria (peptidoglycan). Animal cells have a plasma membrane but no cell wall — a distinction examiners test directly, so never say an animal cell has 'no membrane'.

faq.q: Why do mitochondria and chloroplasts have their own DNA and 70S ribosomes?
faq.a: Because they are thought to be descended from once free-living prokaryotes that were engulfed by a larger cell and kept — the endosymbiotic theory. Their circular DNA, 70S ribosomes and double membranes are the fingerprints of that prokaryotic ancestry, which is why these two organelles can make some of their own proteins. For A2.2 you only need the structural facts; the evolutionary story is background that helps them stick.

faq.q: Can I see all the organelles with a light microscope?
faq.a: No. A light microscope resolves the nucleus, cytoplasm, cell wall and large vacuoles or chloroplasts, but its resolution is limited by the wavelength of light. The fine ultrastructure — mitochondria, ER, Golgi apparatus and individual ribosomes — is only visible with the far higher resolution of an electron microscope, which is why detailed micrographs in exams are electron micrographs.

faq.q: Why can't a cell just keep growing to store more materials?
faq.a: Because supply is limited by surface area but demand is set by volume, and volume grows faster than surface area. As a cell enlarges its surface-area-to-volume ratio falls, so eventually the membrane cannot bring in nutrients and remove wastes fast enough for the volume it must serve. Cells stay small (or adopt flat or folded shapes) to keep a high SA:V; this is why they divide rather than swell, and why exchange surfaces in the body are folded and thin.

sections.content: Almost everything else in biology rests on one idea: that living things are built from cells, and that a cell's structure tells you what it does. This lesson lays that foundation. We start with cell theory and the sharp divide between simple prokaryotic cells and compartmentalised eukaryotic cells, then tour the main organelles by their functions and pin down exactly what separates a plant cell from an animal cell. The second half is quantitative and heavily examined: reading a scale bar to calculate magnification and actual size, and using the surface-area-to-volume ratio to explain why a cell cannot grow without limit. Because A2.2 mixes recall, calculation and explanation, every worked example below is set out the way marks are actually awarded — one mark per distinct, creditable point — so you can see how the way you write an answer decides how many marks you keep.
Cell theory

Cell theory is the founding generalisation of biology, built up from centuries of microscope observations. It has three tenets: all living organisms are composed of one or more cells; the cell is the smallest unit of life; and all cells arise from pre-existing cells by division. The theory is supported by the observation that no smaller unit than a cell shows all the functions of life, and that spontaneous generation was disproved — cells only ever come from other cells. A few atypical cases stretch the definition rather than break it: skeletal muscle fibres are single cells with many nuclei, giant algae can be enormous, and fungal hyphae can have continuous cytoplasm — useful examples to know, but the three tenets remain the answer examiners expect.

items: All living organisms are composed of one or more cells.
The cell is the smallest unit of life — it shows all the functions of life.
All cells arise from pre-existing cells by division.
Atypical examples (multinucleate muscle fibres, giant algae, fungal hyphae) test the limits of the theory but do not overturn it.

sections.content: Prokaryotic versus eukaryotic cell structure

All cells share four features: a plasma membrane, cytoplasm, ribosomes and DNA. The great divide is what a cell does with that DNA and whether it compartmentalises its interior. Prokaryotic cells (bacteria and archaea) are small and simple: their single circular chromosome of 'naked' DNA lies free in the cytoplasm in a region called the nucleoid, and they have NO membrane-bound organelles at all. Their ribosomes are the smaller 70S type, and outside the plasma membrane they have a cell wall (peptidoglycan in bacteria), sometimes a protective capsule, hair-like pili for attachment, and perhaps a flagellum for movement. Eukaryotic cells (plants, animals, fungi and protists) are larger and defined by compartmentalisation: their DNA is enclosed in a membrane-bound nucleus, and the cytoplasm is divided by internal membranes into organelles, each providing specialised conditions for a particular job. This division of labour lets incompatible reactions run at once and raises overall efficiency.

items: **Shared by all cells:** plasma membrane, cytoplasm, ribosomes, DNA.
**Prokaryotic:** no membrane-bound organelles; DNA free in the nucleoid; 70S ribosomes; cell wall of peptidoglycan; small (typically 1–5 μm).
**Eukaryotic:** DNA in a membrane-bound nucleus; membrane-bound organelles (compartmentalisation); 80S ribosomes in the cytoplasm; larger (typically 10–100 μm).
**Compartmentalisation** keeps incompatible processes separate and increases efficiency — the defining advantage of eukaryotic organisation.

sections.content: The main organelles and their functions

The single most useful habit in this topic is to link each organelle to its job, because exam questions rarely ask 'what is this?' — they ask 'what does it do?' or 'how is its structure adapted to its function?'. Work through the organelles below as a set of structure-function pairs.

items: **Nucleus:** stores DNA and controls cell activities; bounded by a double membrane (nuclear envelope) with pores; contains the nucleolus, which makes ribosomes.
**Ribosomes:** site of protein synthesis; found in ALL cells, free in the cytoplasm or on rough ER (80S in eukaryotic cytoplasm, 70S in prokaryotes, mitochondria and chloroplasts).
**Rough endoplasmic reticulum (rER):** studded with ribosomes; synthesises and modifies proteins for secretion or for membranes.
**Smooth endoplasmic reticulum (sER):** no ribosomes; synthesises lipids and steroids and detoxifies substances.
**Golgi apparatus:** modifies, sorts and packages proteins and lipids into vesicles for secretion or delivery.
**Mitochondrion:** site of aerobic respiration and ATP production; double membrane, inner membrane folded into cristae; has its own 70S ribosomes and DNA.
**Chloroplast (plant/algal cells only):** site of photosynthesis; double membrane; contains thylakoids (with chlorophyll) and stroma; own 70S ribosomes and DNA.
**Vacuole:** membrane-bound sac; in plants, one large central vacuole stores cell sap and maintains turgor pressure.
**Cell wall:** rigid layer outside the plasma membrane giving shape and support (cellulose in plants, peptidoglycan in bacteria); absent in animal cells.
**Plasma membrane:** partially permeable phospholipid bilayer present in every cell; controls what enters and leaves.

sections.content: Differences between plant and animal cells

Plant and animal cells are both eukaryotic and share the nucleus, mitochondria, ribosomes, endoplasmic reticulum, Golgi apparatus and plasma membrane. What sets them apart is a small, specific list. Plant cells have a cellulose cell wall, chloroplasts and a large permanent central vacuole; animal cells have none of these, but often contain centrioles (used in cell division) and only small, temporary vacuoles. The trap to avoid is over-generalising from one difference: an animal cell lacks a cell WALL, but it still has a plasma membrane — losing that distinction is a classic dropped mark.

items: **Only in plant cells:** cellulose cell wall, chloroplasts, large permanent central vacuole.
**Typically in animal cells:** centrioles; small temporary vacuoles; no wall and no chloroplasts.
**In both:** nucleus, mitochondria, ribosomes, rough and smooth ER, Golgi apparatus, plasma membrane.
An animal cell has NO cell wall but DOES have a plasma membrane — keep the two structures distinct.

sections.question: The diagram of a cell shows a rigid outer layer, a large fluid-filled central sac and several green disc-shaped organelles. State whether this is a plant or animal cell, and give TWO functions carried out by the labelled organelles: (a) the green disc-shaped organelles, (b) the large central sac. [3]
sections.solution:
**Identifying the cell.** The rigid outer layer (cell wall), the large central vacuole and the green organelles (chloroplasts) are all features unique to plant cells, so this is a PLANT cell. [A1: correctly identifies plant cell, justified by any of these features]

**(a) Green disc-shaped organelles = chloroplasts.** Function: site of photosynthesis — they use light energy to convert carbon dioxide and water into glucose. [A1]

**(b) Large central sac = the (central) vacuole.** Function: stores cell sap and maintains turgor pressure, which supports the cell and helps keep the plant rigid. [A1]

*This is an 'identify the organelle by its function' question — the kind A2.2 loves. Marks are for the JOB (photosynthesis; turgor/support), not for merely naming the organelle. Always give the function, not just the label.*

sections.content: Microscopy: calculating magnification and actual size

Micrographs come with either a stated magnification or, more often, a scale bar — a short line labelled with the real length it represents. You must be able to move between three quantities: the image size (what you measure with a ruler), the actual size (the real size of the structure) and the magnification (how many times larger the image is). They are linked by a single formula, and the one rule that decides most marks is that image size and actual size must be in the same unit before you divide.
\text{magnification} = \frac{\text{image size}}{\text{actual size}}

Rearranged, actual size = image size ÷ magnification. Magnification is a ratio and has no units. Remember the unit ladder: 1 mm = 1000 μm and 1 μm = 1000 nm, so it is usually easiest to convert every length to micrometres (μm) before substituting.

Always show your working on calculation questions, even when the answer looks obvious. Marks are commonly split between stating the formula, converting units correctly and completing the arithmetic — so a slip in one step still leaves the others creditable under error-carried-forward. Write the formula, convert to a common unit, substitute, then give the answer (with 'x' for magnification, or a unit for a size).

sections.question: An electron micrograph carries a scale bar labelled 5 μm. The scale bar measures 20 mm long on the page. A chloroplast in the same image measures 32 mm across. (a) Calculate the magnification of the image. (b) Calculate the actual width of the chloroplast. [4]
sections.solution:
**(a) Magnification from the scale bar.**
Convert the image length of the scale bar to the same unit as its real length: 20 mm = 20 000 μm.
$\text{magnification} = \dfrac{\text{image size}}{\text{actual size}} = \dfrac{20\,000\ \mu m}{5\ \mu m} = 4000\times$.  [M1: correct unit conversion; A1: 4000×]

**(b) Actual width of the chloroplast.**
Rearrange: actual size = image size ÷ magnification. Convert the chloroplast image width: 32 mm = 32 000 μm.
$\text{actual size} = \dfrac{32\,000\ \mu m}{4000} = 8\ \mu m$.  [M1: correct rearrangement and substitution; A1: 8 μm]

**Final answers:** magnification = 4000×; actual chloroplast width = 8 μm.

*Note the scale bar does the work: it lets you find the magnification without being told it. If part (a) had been wrong, the method in (b) would still earn the method mark by error-carried-forward — another reason to show every step.*

sections.content: Surface-area-to-volume ratio and the limit on cell size

Everything a cell needs — oxygen, nutrients, and the removal of wastes and heat — must cross its surface, the plasma membrane. But the demand for those exchanges depends on how much living material there is, which is set by the cell's volume. The problem is geometric: as a cell grows, its volume increases faster than its surface area, so the surface-area-to-volume ratio (SA:V) falls. Beyond a certain size the membrane simply cannot supply the interior fast enough, and the cell cannot survive. This is why cells stay small and divide rather than swell, and why exchange surfaces in organisms are folded and flattened to keep SA:V high. The quickest way to see it is with cubes.
\text{SA:V ratio} = \frac{\text{surface area}}{\text{volume}}

sections.question: Model three cells as cubes of side length 1 cm, 2 cm and 4 cm. Calculate the surface area, volume and surface-area-to-volume ratio of each, and explain what the trend shows about cell size. [4]
sections.solution:
For a cube of side $L$: surface area = $6L^2$ (six faces), volume = $L^3$.

**Cube of side 1 cm:** SA = $6(1)^2 = 6\ cm^2$; V = $1^3 = 1\ cm^3$; SA:V = $6 \div 1 = 6\!:\!1$.  [M1: correct SA and V for one cube]

**Cube of side 2 cm:** SA = $6(2)^2 = 24\ cm^2$; V = $2^3 = 8\ cm^3$; SA:V = $24 \div 8 = 3\!:\!1$.  [A1: correct ratios across the cubes]

**Cube of side 4 cm:** SA = $6(4)^2 = 96\ cm^2$; V = $4^3 = 64\ cm^3$; SA:V = $96 \div 64 = 1.5\!:\!1$.

**What the trend shows.** As the cube's side doubles, SA:V falls from 6:1 to 3:1 to 1.5:1 — the ratio DECREASES as size increases, because volume ($L^3$) grows faster than surface area ($L^2$). [M1: identifies volume rising faster than surface area]  A cell relies on its surface to exchange materials but on its volume to set demand, so a larger cell has proportionally too little surface to supply its interior — this sets an upper limit on cell size. [A1: links falling SA:V to the limit on cell size]

*Numbers first, then the biological meaning. The examiner wants both: the calculated ratios AND the sentence explaining why a falling SA:V limits how large a cell can grow.*

sections.content: Common mistakes examiners penalise

items: **Dividing before converting units** — image and actual size must be in the SAME unit before you apply the magnification formula; a missed mm→μm step throws the answer out by a factor of 1000.
**Getting the magnification formula upside down** — magnification = image ÷ actual. Dividing actual by image gives a value less than 1 for a magnified image, which should ring an alarm.
**Putting a unit on magnification** — magnification is a ratio and has no units; write it as, for example, 4000× (not 4000 μm).
**Reversing the SA:V trend** — the ratio DECREASES as a cell gets larger. Saying it increases with size reverses the whole argument.
**Explaining SA:V with only half the story** — you must state that exchange depends on surface area while demand depends on volume; quoting the ratio without this link earns little credit.
**Saying prokaryotes have 'no organelles'** — they lack MEMBRANE-BOUND organelles, but they do have ribosomes (70S). Ribosomes are found in all cells.
**Claiming an animal cell 'has no membrane'** — it lacks a cell WALL, not the plasma membrane; every cell has a plasma membrane.
**Naming an organelle instead of its function** — 'mitochondrion' is not an answer to 'what does this do?'; the mark is for the JOB (aerobic respiration / ATP production).
**Confusing chloroplast with mitochondrion** — chloroplasts do photosynthesis and are only in plant/algal cells; mitochondria do aerobic respiration and are in nearly all eukaryotic cells.

sections.content: Model answer — marked the way our engine marks it

A2.2 explanation questions are marked analytically: each distinct valid biological point is worth one mark, up to the maximum available. Method-style reasoning points are credited as they appear, an accuracy/answer mark (A) rewards the correct conclusion, and error-carried-forward (ECF) means an early slip does not cost you the marks that follow, as long as your reasoning is written down. Notice below how every mark is pinned to a specific, named idea — not to loose phrasing.

sections.question: Explain, with reference to the surface-area-to-volume ratio, why cells cannot grow indefinitely large. [4]
sections.solution:
**Model answer.**
As a cell grows larger, its volume increases faster than its surface area, so its surface-area-to-volume ratio decreases. The rate at which a cell can exchange materials (take in nutrients and oxygen, remove wastes) depends on the area of its surface membrane, whereas the cell's demand for those materials depends on its volume. Because volume outgrows surface area, a large cell has proportionally too little membrane to supply its interior. Beyond a certain size the surface can no longer meet the demands of the volume, so the cell cannot survive — this sets an upper limit on cell size, which is why cells stay small and divide rather than continue to enlarge.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 4 marks** — each distinct valid point scores 1 (maximum 4):

- **Point 1:** as size increases the surface-area-to-volume ratio DECREASES (volume grows faster than surface area).
- **Point 2:** the rate of exchange of materials depends on the SURFACE AREA (membrane).
- **Point 3:** the demand for materials depends on the VOLUME of the cell.
- **Point 4 (A):** therefore a large cell cannot exchange materials fast enough to meet its demand, so cell size is limited (the conclusion of the reasoning chain).

**Accepted equivalents.** Any correct wording of a point earns it — 'the ratio of surface to volume falls', 'exchange is limited by membrane area', 'requirements scale with the amount of cytoplasm/volume', or a worked SA:V comparison of two cubes showing the ratio dropping — all count. A candidate who supports the argument with numbers (e.g. 6:1 falling to 3:1) is credited for the relevant point.

**ECF and partial credit.** The four points are independent, so a student who omits one still scores the others — there is no all-or-nothing penalty. If an earlier calculated ratio is wrong but the reasoning built on it is sound, the reasoning marks are still awarded by error-carried-forward.

**What the engine rejects.** 'The cell gets too big and dies', or quoting 'SA:V decreases' with no link to exchange versus demand, scores at most one mark — it names the ratio but not the mechanism. Marks are for DISTINCT ideas: stating the ratio falls and then restating it in other words scores once.

**Bottom line:** to secure all 4 marks, hit three separate mechanism points (ratio decreases → exchange depends on surface area → demand depends on volume) and state the conclusion (supply cannot meet demand, so size is limited).

sections.content: The Practice button marks YOUR answer this same way — point by point, with error-carried-forward and a cap at the marks available. So write in distinct, creditable statements: say the SA:V ratio decreases, that exchange depends on surface area, that demand depends on volume, and then draw the conclusion that supply cannot meet demand. Vague sentences like 'it gets too big' earn nothing; the mark scheme rewards each named step, and showing them all is what protects your score.
Where this leads

The ideas in A2.2 seed much of the course ahead. The structure-function pairing of organelles sets up membrane transport and the endomembrane pathway of protein secretion; the prokaryote-eukaryote divide underpins classification and the endosymbiotic origin of mitochondria and chloroplasts; and the surface-area-to-volume argument reappears wherever biology needs an efficient exchange surface — the folded villi of the gut, the branching alveoli of the lungs, the flattened red blood cell. Master the habit of reading structure as a clue to function, and of checking whether a size problem is really a surface-area-to-volume problem, and you have a template that keeps paying off across the whole syllabus.
